Ensure all grounds are fully saturated when starting the steep

south_indian_filter: Ensure beans are fresh—stale coffee loses its natural sweetness
Grind slightly finer to extract more natural sugars

Grind slightly coarser to reduce the amount of microscopic coffee dust
Do not press aggressively on the plunger disc, this forces fines through the holes

Avoid squeezing or pressing the coffee grounds bag when removing it

south_indian_filter: Ensure the metal brewer is thoroughly clean and completely dry before use
Check your beans—woody flavors often indicate stale, old, or over-roasted coffee
Store your coffee in an airtight container away from light and moisture
cold_brew: If using paper filters for final straining, rinse them thoroughly with hot water first
Ensure your steeping vessel is perfectly clean before starting the brew
Old or stale beans heavily impart woody notes in cold brew; use fresher coffee

south_indian_filter: Increase the amount of coffee used relative to the water to build more body

Ensure your water is hot enough (just off the boil) to fully extract the coffee
cold_brew: Use a stronger ratio of coffee to water
Grind finer to increase the overall extraction depth and body
Increase the steeping time to pull out more complex flavor compounds

south_indian_filter.notes: A gravity-fed metal filter method with no agitation, producing a strong concentrate often paired with chicory.

cold_brew.notes: A cold-water immersion method where ratio and time are the primary levers, with absolutely no temperature control.

ratio_range_guide.south_indian_filter: 1:5 to 1:8 (coffee:water by weight for the decoction)
